---
title: "Methods"
author: "Cheyenne Leila Goodman"
---


## Water Table Methods

Eight *Porites astreoides* (PAST) from Brewers Bay at relative depths (4.57-6.09m) were be fragmented into 10-5cm\^2 coral nubbins (n=32). Coral nubbins were be equally divided between ambient water temperature \~28 C and heated water temperature (\~32C). The heated water table rose to 32C for ten days. Ambient and bleached fragments were placed into the randomly field.

## Field Methods

![Figure No. 1. A photo of a volunteer diver, Emma Dlutkowski, taken by Cheyenne L. Goodman](PHOTOS/DIVER.JPG)

My site location is Brewers Bay, east of the Brewers Bay Territorial Coral Reef Monitoring Program (TCRMP) site. I had 18 *Orbicella annularis* dominated plots: 6 damselfish-free control plots, 6 damselfish infested *Orbicella annularis* plots, and 6 damselfish infested *Orbicella annularis* plots where damselfish was removed.

Biweekly, two to three divers will dive (\<25ft) at Brewers Bay (Figure No. 1) to collect damselfishfish. Once a month I  collected data by scanning each 1m\^2 plot with an underwater camera. This process was repeated for 10 weeks.

The camera footage was uploaded into Agisoft Metashape Professional to be converted into a 3D model. 

I plan to take surface interval data of the corals inside the 1m\^2. I will be taking measurements of coral size, lesion size, and benthic percentage. 


![Figure No. 2. A photo of *Orbicella annularis* taken by Cheyenne L. Goodman](PHOTOS/GOODMAN_SCIENCE.JPG)

### Damselfish Collection

All species of damselfish are required to be removed from designated plots (Figure No. 2.). They are to be released to Perseverance Bay after capture.

## Background data

I am reviewing the Territorial Coral Reef Monitoring Program (TCRMP) dataset from 2013-2025 to investigate the frequency of coral-damselfish interactions, damselfish density changes, and macroalgal cover and height across the USVI. See statistical analysis to view these trends between 2013-2025.

I am also reviewing the National Coral Reef Monitoring Program (NCRMP) to look beyond TCRMP sites to gain a better overview of damselfish density in the US Virgin Islands.
